econSignals.label: Fastest Growing Sector: ICT & Digital Services
econSignals.description: Ghana's ICT sector grew at 11.2% per year from 2018–2023, outpacing all other sectors. Mobile money agent and digital freelance work are the fastest entry points.

econSignals.label: Agro-processing Sector Employment Growth
econSignals.description: Cocoa processing, shea butter, and food packaging have driven strong employment growth. Most entry-level roles require technical skills, not degrees.

econSignals.label: Median Monthly Earnings — ICT / Tech Repair
econSignals.description: Skilled electronics repair and IT support workers in Accra earn a median of ~GHS 3,500/month ($280 USD). Range: $180–$480 depending on specialisation.

econSignals.label: Median Monthly Earnings — Market Trade & Retail
econSignals.description: Self-employed traders in Accra's informal markets earn a median of ~GHS 1,800/month ($145 USD). Highly variable by sector and location.

econSignals.label: Returns to Upper Secondary Education
econSignals.source: World Bank Human Capital Index / WDI
econSignals.description: Workers with upper secondary (SHS/WASSCE) credentials earn ~28% more than primary-only workers in Ghana. Post-secondary adds a further 45% premium.

econSignals.label: Youth NEET Rate (not in education, employment, or training)
econSignals.description: Over 1 in 4 Ghanaian youth aged 15–24 are not in education, employment, or training — representing a major pool of unrecognised skill and untapped potential.

econSignals.label: Formal Employment Share
econSignals.description: Only 15% of Ghana's workforce is in formal employment — meaning the vast majority of opportunity lies in supported self-employment, informal sector, and gig work.
